topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. I stared at the screen, the words blurring into an indecipherable mess. "We regret to inform you that your application for the prestigious Rhodes Scholarship has not been selected for this cycle." My hands started to tremble, the cheap plastic pen I was gripping slipping from my fingers onto the desk. I reread the rejection letter, each sentence a sharp splinter digging into my chest. A strange, cold numbness began to spread from my core outward, leaving me feeling strangely disconnected from the room. I pushed back from the desk, the chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor, and walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to breathe in air that suddenly felt too thin.

[story 2]

I reread the final decision, the digital font mocking me with its sterile precision. My mouth went dry, a sudden, alarming tightness constricting my throat. I kept my eyes glued to the screen, refusing to look away, but the colors seemed to drain from the rest of the room. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my stomach clench violently. I stood up abruptly, knocking my backpack to the floor with a dull thud. My breath hitched, a ragged, shallow sound in the silence of my cramped dorm room. I began pacing, a frantic, tight circuit around my small space, my movements jerky and uncontrolled, as if something inside me was vibrating with distress.

[story 3]

When the notification finally registered, the world seemed to tilt. I dropped the phone onto the worn rug, the sound swallowed instantly by the sudden, vast emptiness in my chest. I didn't move for a long time, just remained frozen, staring at the ceiling where the plaster seemed to mock my failure. My vision swam, the edges of the room softening and blurring into an indistinct wash of gray. Slowly, painstakingly, I managed to raise a hand, my fingers brushing against my own cheek as if confirming I was still physically present. A low, involuntary sound escaped my lips—a weak, choked gasp—and I curled inward, pulling my knees tightly to my chest, trying to make myself as small and unnoticeable as possible.
